current_fact.fact_text: On this day in 2002, scientists logged the Mindanao quake’s waves from sensors around the world, turning destruction into data. Each tremor became a clue, helping seismologists map hidden faults and better understand how the planet stores and suddenly releases immense energy.

episode.title: Morning Drops #42 - The Joy Of Learning
episode.description:
Hosted by Yasmin, this Morning Drop explores learning as a lifelong process, focusing on persistence, repetition, and the emotional landscape that comes with challenging oneself over time.

Through personal stories and scientific insights, the episode looks at how learning reshapes the brain, generates joy through effort, and why progress often arrives after confusion and frustration. It reflects on the so-called delayed pleasure effect, the role of dopamine in motivation, and how curiosity and learning support long-term wellbeing.

The episode also questions our cultural relationship with failure and invites a softer, more realistic approach to learning, one that accepts mistakes as part of growth and sees persistence as a quiet form of strength.

Mood: reflective, grounded, quietly hopeful
Music genre: alternative rock, hip hop, electronic, neo-soul, drill

A space to listen slowly and stay with the process.

current_fact.match_reason: Transforming quake chaos into scientific understanding mirrors the episode’s theme of turning confusion and struggle into long-term learning and growth.

published.fact_text: On March 5, 1979, three spacecraft—Venera 11, Venera 12, and the Helios II solar probe—were all blasted by an "off the scale" flash of gamma rays at nearly the same time. What looked like an instrument glitch turned out to be the first detection of soft gamma repeaters, mysterious cosmic objects that repeatedly emit powerful gamma-ray bursts.

episode.title: Morning Drops #03 - Space Shift
episode.description: Gaia invites you to shift your mindset with a simple yet powerful reflection: What if we saw emptiness not as a void but as space to create? An energising and feel-good selection of tracks, the perfect way to ease into your day and set the tone for the week ahead.
songs: Willy J Healey - Woke Up Smiling
The Strokes - You Only Live Once

published.match_reason: A cosmic gamma-ray mystery fits the episode’s theme of reimagining space as possibility, with energetic tracks echoing the sudden, explosive nature of the discovery.

published.fact_text: On this day in 1982, the Soviet Venera 14 probe survived a brutal descent into Venus’s atmosphere and landed on a surface hot enough to melt lead. It managed to send back color images and data from a hellish world where the sky is orange, the pressure is 90 times Earth’s, and even spacecraft are crushed in under an hour.

episode.title: Sedna FM #02 - Destination Sedna
episode.description: Continuing the Sedna FM journey together with family and friends! Episode two of the main series — exploring new musical territories and deepening the connection with our listeners. A collective voyage through sound.
songs: Odion Iruoje - Identify With Your Roots
Depeche mode - world in my eyes (Oil Tank Mix)

published.match_reason: A perilous descent to Venus mirrors the idea of a deep, exploratory voyage through new sonic territories in this episode.

published.fact_text: Landsat 3 was launched on March 5, 1978, quietly extending humanity’s long-term “memory” of Earth from orbit. With its sensors, it recorded patterns of forests, cities, oceans, and deserts—creating a time-lapse of planetary change that scientists still use to study how our world is evolving.

episode.title: Morning Drops #14 - Slow Planet
episode.description: A pause. A quiet return to slower rhythms, sparked by a live set under the summer sky. A moment to breathe and settle into the week.

published.match_reason: An Earth-observing satellite that lets us watch the planet slowly change pairs well with a contemplative ‘Slow Planet’ soundtrack of chilled, expansive electronics.

published.fact_text: On March 5, 1616, the Catholic Church placed Nicolaus Copernicus’s "On the Revolutions of the Heavenly Spheres" on its Index of Forbidden Books—73 years after it proposed that Earth orbits the Sun. The book that quietly rewired our view of the cosmos was officially branded too dangerous for ordinary readers.

episode.title: Morning Drops #27 - Nomophobia
episode.description: Yasmin reflects on what happened when she recently lost her phone and why it felt freeing. A dive into the psychology of our relationship with smartphones: from nomophobia (the fear of being without a phone), to digital burnout and cognitive overload, to the surprising benefits of boredom and the brain's default mode network. An invitation to rethink how we connect and disconnect.

published.match_reason: Censoring a book that changed how we see the universe echoes the episode’s theme of questioning the systems and technologies that quietly shape our reality.

published.fact_text: On this day in 1496, King Henry VII issued letters patent to John Cabot, granting him permission to sail under the English flag in search of “unknown lands.” This document helped launch one of the earliest English-sponsored voyages across the Atlantic, setting the stage for future transoceanic exploration.

episode.title: Morning Drops #31 - Sailing With Germans
episode.description: From the deck of a sailing boat, hosted by Yasmin and her special guests. A week at sea with six Germans, each with their own stories, songs, and ways of moving through the waves. From shared meals to unexpected laughter, from quiet resilience to sun-soaked joy — a collection of voices and tracks that reflect what it means to journey together.
songs: Ben Howard - Keep Your Head Up

published.match_reason: A royal charter for Atlantic exploration resonates with a sea-bound episode literally recorded on a sailing boat, both celebrating life carried by waves and wind.

published.fact_text: On March 5, 1868, Arrigo Boito’s opera "Mefistofele" premiered at La Scala in Milan, offering a bold, atmospheric retelling of Goethe’s Faust. The initial performance was so long and unconventional that it scandalized audiences and censors, forcing Boito to radically revise the work before it found lasting success.

episode.title: Sedna FM #04 - Queen of Spades
episode.description: Tuning in from the planet of irrational love. Exploring love's most vulnerable side — the kind that awakens the child within us. A diverse music selection ranging from electric blues to downtempo, soul, and jazz. A tribute to the feeling that has moved artists to create the most beautiful works. Love makes us vulnerable, and there is no art without vulnerability.
songs: Alabama Shakes - Gimme All Your Love
Queens of the stone age - Make it wit chu
Etta James - I'd Rather Go Blind
Eric Clapton - Have you ever loved a woman

published.match_reason: An opera about a pact with the devil and dangerous passion fits the episode’s deep dive into vulnerable, intoxicating love and its artistic fallout.

published.fact_text: On this day in 1811, at the Battle of Barrosa in the Peninsular War, a smaller Anglo-Spanish-Portuguese force unexpectedly routed French troops under Marshal Victor. The victory, won on sand dunes and pine-covered ridges near Cádiz, helped secure the besieged city and became a symbol of stubborn resistance against Napoleon.

episode.title: Morning Drops #49 - Rage Against The Machine
episode.description:
In this Morning Drop, Yasmin revisits the story of Rage Against the Machine — a band born in tension, shaped by conviction, and remembered for moments that blurred the line between stage and protest.
From early Los Angeles unrest to Wall Street performances, this episode moves through rupture, resistance, and legacy — carried by songs that still feel urgent today.
Listen closely.
songs: Rage Against the Machine - Killing in the Name
Rage Against the Machine - Without a Face
Rage Against the Machine - Guerrilla Radio
Rage Against the Machine - Sleep Now in the Fire

published.match_reason: A gritty battlefield victory against imperial power aligns with Rage Against the Machine’s sound of defiance and resistance to oppression.

published.fact_text: On March 5, 1825, Roberto Cofresí—one of the last famed pirates of the Caribbean—was defeated in battle and captured by authorities. Beloved by some as a Robin Hood figure and feared by others as a ruthless raider, his downfall marked the fading of the romantic age of sail-era piracy in the region.

episode.title: Morning Drops #22 - Sicilians On The Dolomites
episode.description: Hosted by Yasmin, a musical journey inspired by the mountains. Joined by two special guests, exploring the tracks they've chosen, hearing the stories behind their picks, and discovering the personal memories each song brings to life — a blend of music, voices, and landscapes you can hear.
songs: Florian Pellissier Quintet - Coup de foudre à Thessalonique
Lucio Dalla & Francesco De Gregori - Ma come fanno i marinai

published.match_reason: A legendary Caribbean pirate’s capture pairs nicely with an episode featuring "Ma come fanno i marinai"—a song literally about sailors—blending seafaring myth and reflective storytelling.

published.fact_text: On this day in 363, the Roman emperor Julian marched out of Antioch at the head of some 90,000 troops, beginning a bold invasion of the Sasanian Empire. The campaign, meant to restore Rome’s eastern glory, instead led him deep into hostile territory and ultimately to his own death.

episode.title: Morning Drops #35 - Thailand
episode.description: Recorded in Koh Samui, Thailand, where Yasmin and her friend Patricia spent more than two intense weeks training Muay Thai and Western boxing. Between sweat, rhythm, and sea breeze, they share reflections on discipline, friendship, and the beauty of movement - with a soundtrack that connects Thai sounds to the flow of daily practice.
songs: Burna Boy - Cheat on Me (feat. Dave)

published.match_reason: A grueling imperial campaign echoes this episode’s themes of physical discipline, training, and pushing one’s limits in intense conditions.

published.fact_text: On March 5, 1942, Japanese troops captured Batavia, the capital of the Dutch East Indies (now Jakarta), after Allied forces withdrew. The fall of the city signaled a dramatic shift in control of Southeast Asia’s resources and reshaped the region’s path toward eventual independence movements.

episode.title: Morning Drops #16 - The Sound of Tuareg
episode.description: A journey into the sound of the Tuareg: from traditional chants and desert rituals to the electrified rhythms of exile and resistance. Music shaped by sand, memory, and survival.

published.match_reason: A wartime occupation that later fuels independence struggles mirrors the episode’s focus on music born from exile, survival, and resistance to domination.

published.fact_text: On this day in 1943, a general strike and protest march erupted in Athens against rumors that Greek workers would be forcibly sent to labor in Germany. Despite violent clashes with occupation forces, the protest succeeded: the decree was withdrawn the very next day, a rare victory under brutal conditions.

episode.title: Morning Drops #11 - Mediterranean Boat
episode.description: Gaia invites you to embark on a musical voyage across the Mediterranean. Exploring music from different countries shared by people who call them home. A warm, sun-soaked journey through coastal sounds.
songs: Fabrizio De André - Crêuza de mä

published.match_reason: A Greek-led protest on the Mediterranean shores fits a musical voyage through Mediterranean cultures, with Greek folk setting the emotional tone of collective resistance.

published.fact_text: On March 5, 1940, six members of the Soviet Politburo—including Joseph Stalin—signed an order authorizing the execution of 25,700 Polish prisoners and intelligentsia, an atrocity remembered as the Katyn massacre. The killings were denied for decades, leaving a wound in Polish memory and a chilling example of how bureaucracy can mask mass violence.

episode.title: Morning Drops #20 - Hitchhiking Poland
episode.description: Yasmin takes you back to the summer of 2020, when borders were still uncertain and a long-held wish to visit Poland finally came true. Through four tracks, tracing her journey from a stranger's couch in Poznań to the peaks of Zakopane.

published.match_reason: A painful chapter in Polish history is paired with an episode built around modern Polish journeys, offering a softer, personal counterpoint to a national trauma.

published.fact_text: On this day in 1963, country music stars Patsy Cline, Hawkshaw Hawkins, Cowboy Copas, and their pilot Randy Hughes died in a plane crash near Camden, Tennessee. Cline’s voice had already redefined country’s emotional range, and her sudden death at 30 cemented her as a haunting, enduring icon.

episode.title: Morning Drops #01 - Timeless Legacy
episode.description: Gaia guides us through timeless music to start the morning with the right foot! The first episode of the Morning Drops series — classic sounds that never fade.
songs: Roberta Flack - Killing me softly with his song

published.match_reason: The loss of a country legend whose songs became timeless aligns with an episode explicitly celebrating music that never fades—a quiet salute to artistic legacy.

published.fact_text: On March 5, 1966, BOAC Flight 911, a Boeing 707, broke apart mid-flight near Mount Fuji after encountering violent clear-air turbulence. The invisible, cloudless turbulence was so severe it ripped off a large section of the plane’s fuselage, highlighting how unseen atmospheric forces can suddenly overwhelm engineering.

episode.title: Morning Drops #04 - Electric Shock
episode.description: An energizing episode with electrifying sounds to wake you up and get you moving. High energy tracks that deliver a jolt of musical electricity to start your day.

published.match_reason: The sudden, invisible violence of clear-air turbulence echoes the episode’s theme of a jolt—an ‘electric shock’ of energy—though here it’s nature, not guitars, that overloads the system.

published.fact_text: On this day in 1967, Lake Central Airlines Flight 527 broke apart and crashed near Marseilles, Ohio, killing all 38 people on board. Investigators traced the disaster to the in-flight failure of a propeller that detached and struck the fuselage—an eerie reminder of how a single component can decide the fate of an entire journey.

episode.title: Morning Drops #33 - Immerse
episode.description: An analogy between diving, one of Gaia's great passions, and the act of immersing in life itself. A reflection on what happens when we go below the surface: when we let ourselves be fully present. From moments of calm suspension to warm, rhythmic flow, the selection moves like water — fluid, organic, and alive. An invitation to slow down, listen deeper, and float.
songs: Pino D'Angiò - Un Po' d'Uva e un Liquore

published.match_reason: A flight torn apart by a single failure contrasts sharply with an episode about surrender and safe immersion, inviting reflection on fragility and the technology we trust.

published.fact_text: On March 5, 1973, an Iberia DC-9 collided mid-air with a Spantax Convair 990 near Nantes, France, killing all 68 people on the DC-9, including music manager Michael Jeffery. Jeffery had managed acts like The Animals and Jimi Hendrix, so his death quietly rippled through rock history.

episode.title: Morning Drops #50 - Postcards from Earth-London
episode.description:
Postcards from Earth-London is a Morning Drops walk through the city Gaia has called home for the past ten years, a place made of sound, memory, and late-night streets.
Four tracks guide the journey: from iconic landmarks to quieter corners, from romance to reality checks, and back to the reason the city still feels alive: music.

published.match_reason: A plane crash that claimed a rock manager’s life intersects with an episode steeped in rock history and urban music memory, tying aviation tragedy back to the sound of Earth’s stages.

published.fact_text: On this day in 1970, the Treaty on the Non-Proliferation of Nuclear Weapons (NPT) entered into force after ratification by 43 countries. It was an ambitious attempt to freeze the global spread of nuclear arms, balancing secrecy, power, and the hope that some technologies are too destructive to share freely.

episode.title: Sedna FM #01 - November Bomb
episode.description: The first Family & Friends radio episode from Yasmin Sarbaoui and Gaia Parolini. Where it all began — a life soundtrack that launched Sedna FM into the universe. Raw, heartfelt, and full of the energy that defines everything that followed.
songs: Canserbero - De la vida como pelicula, tragedia, comedia y ficcion

published.match_reason: A global pact about nuclear “bombs” neatly resonates with an episode titled “November Bomb,” both dealing—very differently—with explosive power and the energy we choose to release into the world.

published.fact_text: On March 5, 2002, a magnitude 7.5 earthquake struck off Mindanao in the southern Philippines, killing 15 people and injuring more than 100. The quake triggered landslides and structural collapses, revealing how quickly solid ground can turn treacherous in a tectonically restless region.

episode.title: Morning Drops #45 - It Can't Rain Forever
episode.description:
In this first broadcast of 2026, Gaia returns from the vivid landscapes of Morocco to open a space for reflection. This episode navigates the delicate transition between closing a significant chapter and stepping into the new year. It explores the concept of constancy amidst change, celebrating the grounding force of friendship and the creative sanctuary that is Sedna FM.

The musical selection mirrors this journey, moving from the orchestral depth of changing seasons into the golden warmth of psychedelic soul. It recalls memories of Swiss mountains and unexpected downpours, reminding us that shared optimism can weather any storm.

Take a moment to breathe in the new morning and listen deeply.

Mood: Reflective, Warm, Transitional, Hopeful
Music genre: Classic Rock, Psychedelic Soul, Pop Rock, Soft Rock

published.match_reason: A destructive earthquake that abruptly changes lives fits an episode about weathering storms and believing that upheaval—literal or metaphorical—doesn’t last forever.

published.fact_text: On this day in 2012, Tropical Storm Irina swept past Madagascar, killing more than 75 people as heavy rains and winds triggered floods and landslides. The storm’s looping, unpredictable path across the southwest Indian Ocean showed how even “moderate” cyclones can have deadly, drawn-out impacts.

episode.title: Morning Drops #23 - Morning Dubs
episode.description: Recorded while still processing Houghton Festival. One of the mornings there, waking up in the woods to a reggae DJ set — no phone signal, just trees, music, and loved ones. A slow selection of reggae, dub, and morning rhythms. Dubs instead of drops. Start your day slow — with space to breathe.
songs: The Gaylads - Joy in the Morning
King Tubby - King Tubby Meets the Rockers Uptown

published.match_reason: A looping tropical storm over the Indian Ocean connects naturally with a dub-and-reggae episode—genres born in storm-prone islands and shaped by waves, rain, and resilience.

published.fact_text: On March 5, 2018, amid the Syrian civil war, the Syrian Democratic Forces paused their Deir ez-Zor offensive to respond to Turkey’s invasion of Afrin. The halt revealed how one front line in a conflict can suddenly shift when alliances, priorities, and pressures collide.

episode.title: Morning Drops #44 - Residente
episode.description:
In this edition of Morning Drops, Yasmin navigates the complex geography of René Pérez Joglar, known to the world as Residente. His story is not just a musical evolution; it is a timeline marked by resistance, lyrical obsession, and a relentless search for identity.

From the gated streets of Trujillo Alto to the global stage, we explore how a restless mind transformed into the voice of a generation. The selection moves through the textures of his life: hearing the raw energy of his early rebellion, the sharp precision of his political critique, and finally, the fragile silence of his most personal confessions.

It is a study in duality. We witness the rapper who dismantles giants and the son who misses his mother. It is a soundscape that proves that sometimes, the loudest revolution happens inside.

Mood: Raw, Revolutionary, Introspective, Honest
Music genre: Latin Alternative, Hip Hop, Urban, World

published.match_reason: A frontline pause in a complex, multi-sided war fits an episode about Residente, whose work tracks resistance, geopolitics, and the inner cost of conflict.

published.fact_text: On March 5, 2002, Tropical Storm Irina wasn’t the only force reshaping landscapes—on March 5, 2012, it was still affecting southern Africa—but back on March 5, 2002, the Mindanao quake did the same beneath the sea. Tectonic plates slipped, sending ripples through the Earth that would be recorded and studied worldwide—nature’s own low-frequency drum solo.

episode.title: Morning Drops #25 - A Trip-Hop Journey
episode.description: Hosted by friend Lorenzo, a journey through the roots and evolution of trip-hop. From Bristol's early experiments with Massive Attack and Tricky, to DJ Shadow's cinematic sampling in California, and Thievery Corporation's global downtempo grooves in Washington D.C. — exploring how this genre blurred boundaries and reshaped electronic music.
songs: Tricky - Hell Is Round the Corner
DJ Shadow - Midnight in a Perfect World

published.match_reason: Seismic waves silently rolling through the planet parallel trip-hop’s deep, slow pulses—earthquakes as the Earth’s own downtempo rhythm section.

published.fact_text: On this day in 2012, Tropical Storm Irina’s journey around Madagascar lasted over a week, its spiral path drawn in satellite imagery like a slow, dangerous dance. Each loop brought new rain and wind, turning weather maps into a kind of moving art made of clouds and chaos.

episode.title: Morning Drops #39 - Moving Meditation
episode.description: An episode inspired by how travel helps us see more clearly. Stepping into new places opens the mind, shifts perspective. The music moves through balearic warmth, boogie grooves, world and latin-folk influences, and touches of new wave — a selection built to follow the quiet changes that happen when you're out of your usual rhythm.
songs: España Circo Este - Dormo poco e sogno molto
Talking Heads - This Must Be the Place (Naive Melody)

published.match_reason: Irina’s looping path around an island evokes the episode’s idea of travel-as-meditation: moving in circles, seeing the same place differently with each pass.

published.fact_text: On March 5, 1943, Athens showed that even under occupation, streets could still speak loudly enough to change policy. For one day, rumors of forced labor turned into a collective roar that forced a retreat—not of armies, but of a decree.

episode.title: Morning Drops #04 - Bars With Balls
episode.description: Yasmin dives into Women's Day through the voices of four incredible female rappers. Morning fire from the magic planet of Sedna FM — move your hands up and down with us. Hip hop culture through a feminine lens.
songs: Little Simz - Point and Kill (feat. Obongjayar)

published.match_reason: A mass protest that forces an occupier to back down connects with an episode centered on powerful voices demanding dignity and change through rap.

published.fact_text: On this day in 2002, scientists logged the Mindanao quake’s waves from sensors around the world, turning destruction into data. Each tremor became a clue, helping seismologists map hidden faults and better understand how the planet stores and suddenly releases immense energy.

episode.title: Morning Drops #42 - The Joy Of Learning
episode.description:
Hosted by Yasmin, this Morning Drop explores learning as a lifelong process, focusing on persistence, repetition, and the emotional landscape that comes with challenging oneself over time.

Through personal stories and scientific insights, the episode looks at how learning reshapes the brain, generates joy through effort, and why progress often arrives after confusion and frustration. It reflects on the so-called delayed pleasure effect, the role of dopamine in motivation, and how curiosity and learning support long-term wellbeing.

The episode also questions our cultural relationship with failure and invites a softer, more realistic approach to learning, one that accepts mistakes as part of growth and sees persistence as a quiet form of strength.

Mood: reflective, grounded, quietly hopeful
Music genre: alternative rock, hip hop, electronic, neo-soul, drill

A space to listen slowly and stay with the process.

published.match_reason: Transforming quake chaos into scientific understanding mirrors the episode’s theme of turning confusion and struggle into long-term learning and growth.
